words: ROB : I wanted to ask you about the whole time when your family left
Missouri.
NELLIE SMITH: Well, I found a little picture this morning going through the
dresser darwers in there. And a picture of my sister and myself, and that
was taken in Missouri just before we left and that was about the same time
that that one was taken. But let's see* maybe I can get ahold of pretty
easy. Then we left Missouri when I was just abot I think I must: have been
six there and seven here cause this looks a little bit older than that
does, and they were both made in MisstJri.

speaker: ROB
words: How did you come to leave Missouri?

speaker: NWS
words: Well, Dad did. Dad always kinda had a hankering come out West. He wanted

speaker: NWS
words: to know what this West was all about. He had heard so much about it. And

speaker: NWS
words: so, that a couple of my boys when they were little. That's the second

speaker: NWS
words: and the younger one.

speaker: NWS
words: Those Buster Brown suits?

speaker: NWS
words: Uh huh, yes the little Buster Browns all the way through.

speaker: NWS
words: Your dad had a hankerin to come West.

speaker: NWS
words: He always kinda had a hankerin to know what all this West was like.

speaker: NWS
words: So Mother wasn't very anxious about the idea- but well she didn't kick

speaker: NWS
words: so much except he finally got a chance to go out, you know, for a fella that's

speaker: NWS
words: just working a little bit time here and there, and just odd jobs you don't

speaker: NWS
words: have money enough to trip on very often. So there's a friend of his, they

speaker: NWS
words: were shipping a carload of cattle out there to--I don't know whether they

speaker: NWS
words: was gohg to Denver whether they was going to northern Colorado or Wyoming.

speaker: NWS
words: Anyhow, Dad was supposed to go along with that car of cattle and care for

speaker: NWS
words: them and see that they had water and food and things all along the trip.

speaker: NWS
words: Oh, he was all up in the air that he got a chance to go. Mother didn't want

speaker: NWS
words: him to go very bad. She'd just as sonn he'd stayed out of (chuckles)

speaker: ROB
words: How long was he gdhg to be gone?

speaker: NWS
words: Well, he was just going to go out there, and I think if I remember, he had

speaker: NWS
words: this idea if he could get to work out there you know, he'd stay out there

speaker: NWS
words: and work. Well, he got out there and he did all right on his trip, and

speaker: NWS
words: then got work right away on the railroad. There was a wonderful man

speaker: NWS
words: that run the railroad, he got acquainted with him. It was Mr. Lee, I guess

speaker: NWS
words: his name was. Anyhow he was the foreman of that carpenter gang. And

speaker: NWS
words: Dad was on the carpenter building bridges, bridge gang. So he went to work

speaker: NWS
words: there and wrote back Mother that he'd gotten a job. And he said he'd wait

speaker: NWS
words: there for, you know, to get a little stake ahead and he'd send for us.

speaker: NWS
words: And Mother pretty near didn't get to go. She'd been pretty near getting

speaker: NWS
words: killed just after he had left. And my aunt was one of her younger sisters

speaker: NWS
words: and was to be married soon. And she was having a big wedding and it

speaker: NWS
words: was at home but she had this big wedding. And well, they were sewing on her

speaker: NWS
words: dress on all the fancy frills that were to go on it and they ran out of

speaker: NWS
words: lace to finish it up with. So her name Chloe, the one that was to get

speaker: NWS
words: married. But Mother Amy and her younger sister was Lenora,

speaker: NWS
words: and they went to town one morning and hitched up the buggy and little

speaker: NWS
words: team. They always went to town for anything they needed. They lived five,

speaker: NWS
words: six miles out, I guess. And they went down, oh just a little ways, within

speaker: NWS
words: tho eight of the house. It wasn't more than about a block or so from

speaker: NWS
words: our house. And they would go right along the edge of the fence and they

speaker: NWS
words: had to go down a little dip across a little ditch that lead to bridges

speaker: NWS
words: over the creek. And the boys had trimmed up the orchard and put all the

speaker: NWS
words: trimmings of the orchard, threw r over in that creek down there. I

speaker: NWS
words: don't know how come, but that's where they were anyhow. And they went over

speaker: NWS
words: this way and down across the bridge and then go up again and along on their

speaker: NWS
words: way. Well, when they got down here and crossed the bridge and they started

speaker: NWS
words: up there again they--what is it? The neck yoke or whatever the front

speaker: NWS
words: part, the harness goes across 'em here. That hitch someway or other or

speaker: NWS
words: something broke about it, and turned the buggy loose and it just rolled right

speaker: NWS
words: down the ridge and off over the side and dumped them right out of that

speaker: NWS
words: buggy and into that bunch of limbs and prunings. And they thought her neck

speaker: NWS
words: was broken for a while. Then there was a stick that ran right into her ear

speaker: NWS
words: and punctured the e% drum, one of those limb s, those sticks. And she was

speaker: NWS
words: unconscious for two three, four days there at the home. The other sister

speaker: NWS
words: came running back in, and it didn't hurt her. She came running back in for

speaker: NWS
words: help I guess one of them went to the doctor and the other brought her on in

speaker: NWS
words: in the house And she was unconscious for several days and she finally came

speaker: NWS
words: out of it though. But she never could hear out of that one ear. So that was

speaker: NWS
words: kinfl of an offset. But I don't remember about that wedding. Maybe we left

speaker: NWS
words: before tha wedding, I don't know. But I don't think we did becaus3 that wasn't

speaker: NWS
words: very long until the wedding. I don't think ihat they would be doing all thfe

speaker: NWS
words: rushing and fussing around. So Dad worked there for awhile until, well

speaker: NWS
words: he figured he had a paycheck or two and then he was—I think he had towrk

speaker: NWS
words: abther month or something like that before he was entitled to a pass on the

speaker: NWS
words: railroad. And as soon as he filled in his time he sent the pass back and

speaker: NWS
words: said,"Come on." And so.

speaker: ROB
words: How did your momt feel about that?

speaker: NWS
words: Oh, well she felt all right. She'd go try it and see if it was all right,

speaker: NWS
words: you know. So we went out and landed in Laramie, Wyoming.

speaker: ROB
words: Did you want to come?

speaker: NWS
words: I just wanted to be where the folks were; I wasn't old enough to think

speaker: NWS
words: it made much difference Although I don't remember anything at all. Might

speaker: NWS
words: as well skip it, I don't remember anything about tiat. But I suppose that

speaker: NWS
words: we kinda hated to leave grandmother and all those folks. There was eight

speaker: NWS
words: of their kids, they were all grown up at that time--all grown and

speaker: NWS
words: grandmother and grandfather. But still I guess I was anxious to see Dad too.

speaker: NWS
words: So I guess it wasn't making much difference to me a* that time.

speaker: ROB
words: Do you remember the train ride out?

speaker: NWS
words: No, I guess I don't do that either. After we lived at Wyoming awhile I

speaker: NWS
words: remember parts of the town. I remember we lived just about two blocks from

speaker: NWS
words: the university there. And I remember the Wickter girl. What was her first

speaker: NWS
words: name? But last name was Wickter. And so Mother asked tier, if she'd care

speaker: NWS
words: to take me over to school with her when she went to school the next day.

speaker: NWS
words: And she said,"Yes." And she didn't want me to go alone, she wanted the girl

speaker: NWS
words: to look after me on the way and back.I guess it wasn't very far to

speaker: NWS
words: school, but it was a strange place to me, you know. So I went te school

speaker: NWS
words: with her and got used to it. And she'd always come home and they just lived

speaker: NWS
words: right across the street from us. So that was handy.

speaker: ROB
words: You didn't stay there too long, did you?

speaker: NWS
words: Well, we were there a year or more I'm sure because I was, I think, promoted

speaker: NWS
words: to the third grade while I was there. I went to school there, and of course

speaker: NWS
words: they started me in the first grade but I had gone with my aunt to school. I

speaker: NWS
words: had one aunt that was the youngest of the whole bunch of 'em, and she was

speaker: NWS
words: still going to school. She was grown up, she was I suppose fifteen, sixteen

speaker: NWS
words: years old. And she would take me to school with her once in awhile then.

speaker: NWS
words: Well, partly they thought it woiLd be good for me to learn then what school

speaker: NWS
words: was all about. And mother said it was time that I was beginning to learn

speaker: NWS
words: my ABC's. I can just remember them kind of talking about this time I was in

speaker: NWS
words: school. But it was, I think, about a mile to go, something like that. So

speaker: NWS
words: I didn't go much during bad weather anything, I just - some days I'd go and

speaker: NWS
words: some days I didn't. But whatever they said. But if it was a bad day, why they just

speaker: NWS
words: kept me home. They'd take me to school; I was learning these little words to

speaker: NWS
words: read, you know. I think I already knew my A B C's and things. I could count

speaker: NWS
words: and everything . So finally the teacher would take me on his lap, set me on his

speaker: NWS
words: lap, and talk to me about the lesson and teach me. And I was awful embarrassed

speaker: NWS
words: because I thought he thought I was a baby and I just wasn't (laughs). And I

speaker: NWS
words: was kind of - got me - I didn't say anthing but I kind of wished that I didn't

speaker: NWS
words: have to st on his lap. But he just thought he was being real nice to me, you

speaker: NWS
words: know. I guess I was the pet of the whole bunch, the youngest one around there

speaker: NWS
words: and new in the school so. And so I was, oh, reading those little things that they

speaker: NWS
words: do. I could - I do pretty good for, I guess I could do part of that at home. But I

speaker: NWS
words: think I probably knew my A B C's before that,anyhow I was learning. They said

speaker: NWS
words: they thought I was learning fast enough, I wasn't lo sing anything. But anyhow, that's

speaker: NWS
words: in Missouri; that's the first hint of school I had had.

speaker: NWS
words: When I got to Laramie, why they put me there in the first grade. And I

speaker: NWS
words: was in there two weeks and they sent me over to the second grade. So I guess

speaker: NWS
words: I was getting along pretty fast and I was learning that real fast. And when they

speaker: NWS
words: had the music,see, vocal lessons in there and the teacher went and told - I can

speaker: NWS
words: remember just parts of that. I remember them going talking to the other teacher.

speaker: NWS
words: I remember the kids in the room - two rooms. That's clear to me. But she

speaker: NWS
words: told 'em, she said, "I think this little youngster- she's ahead of my grades," and

speaker: NWS
words: she said, "I think she needs to be in the other class because," she said,"I think

speaker: NWS
words: she's able to handle it quite well. We'll put her over there and try her." Put me

speaker: NWS
words: in there and she sold me for the music and I fell for that, put - me- in there, in

speaker: NWS
words: the

speaker: NWS
words: second grade, and when it came time time to - well I just - I eat that up., when it

speaker: NWS
words: came time for the music. Why she'd have us 'do, re, mi', you know,they'd

speaker: NWS
words: listen - training - voice training. And then they'd pick up certain notes and

speaker: NWS
words: sound the cartain and I could do it. An d the other kids - wasn't hardly any other

speaker: NWS
words: kid do it readily. They'd have to make an effort to or a second or so. And I

speaker: NWS
words: could run the scale upwards or backwards or which ever way you wanted it. So

speaker: NWS
words: they got me right on that easy. So I was, I think, I remember right, I was

speaker: NWS
words: getting ready for third grade when I left there. I was promoted. Yeah.

speaker: NWS
words: Then we left out of Laramie and - now I don't know - yes, I do too. There

speaker: NWS
words: was a - Robinson was that - I knew that didn't sound like the name the

speaker: NWS
words: bridge foreman, that boss. His name was Robinson.

speaker: ROB
words: Ohhh.

speaker: NWS
words: His name was Robinson.

speaker: ROB
words: Instead of Lee?

speaker: NWS
words: Instead of Lee. The next people that we got acquainted with were in Rexberg.

speaker: NWS
words: They were Lee. I knew that name didn't sound right but I knew it was Lee

speaker: NWS
words: concerned. That was S.J. Robinson, I think it was. And had a little

speaker: NWS
words: boy about five years old. And his name was Stillman Robinson after his

speaker: NWS
words: daddy. Stillman something-or-other Robinson. So after we'd been there

speaker: NWS
words: a while, well, Dad was still working with the bridge gang. Mrs. Robinson -

speaker: NWS
words: she was there - well, she cooked for the menjou know. They had the

speaker: NWS
words: cars, lived in there in the cars and they were all fixed up real cozy in

speaker: NWS
words: there. But she had a hired girl and I think she was a Swedish girl if I

speaker: NWS
words: remember right. Anyhow, she was a good worker and she used to help

speaker: NWS
words: Mrs. Robinson with all the work. And Mrs. Robinson was a little bit of a

speaker: NWS
words: wiffet - about as big as nothing. And so she said, "I wanted to know," of Dad

speaker: NWS
words: she said, "Why don't you bring your wife out some time? Bring her out to

speaker: NWS
words: visit." She got kind of lonely there and had no chance of getting away to

speaker: NWS
words: visit anybody. And she knew that we hadn't gotten acquainted - didn't know

speaker: NWS
words: many people yet. So he said, well, he would try to do that. And so they

speaker: NWS
words: arranged it. We were to go out and spend Christmas with her - with them there.

speaker: NWS
words: So everything was all set. Dad had been home the weekend before and he

speaker: NWS
words: had made arrangements just how we were going. The regular line.the train

speaker: NWS
words: came along here, but they were putting in a branch line into another place.

speaker: NWS
words: And that's - and it wasn't, oh, it was about a quarter of a mile, I think,

speaker: NWS
words: between that one and this one where he told us to have the conductor let us

speaker: NWS
words: off here at this crossing here and about a quarter of a mile from the other.

speaker: NWS
words: He said they'd stop if you tell them that. And so he took - gave all the instructions

speaker: NWS
words: so we got already and it was winter time, well, it was Christmas anyhow. And so

speaker: NWS
words: we were all outfitted for winter time.

speaker: NWS
words: And we got off^the train here and he put my sister, you see how big she

speaker: NWS
words: was - this little picture. Well, and - she was light too, you know. She's

speaker: NWS
words: always the punny thing. And I was a husky big old - but nothing ever bothered

speaker: NWS
words: me, I was always hard to, you know, nothing seemed to hurt me. Everything

speaker: NWS
words: that came along after I got out where I could... Now before I left Missouri, I

speaker: NWS
words: was about six then, when we had the whooping cough. I don't know who got it

speaker: NWS
words: first but anyhow, Edna , my sister, she was just coughing, just coughing her

speaker: NWS
words: little head off. And she'd just cough till she'd fall over or something. Or soon

speaker: NWS
words: as she'd got used to it so she started to cough she run and grab for a chair and

speaker: NWS
words: hold on to it till mother'd get a hold of her. The doctor sometimes had to come

speaker: NWS
words: a time or two during the day. Pret'near every day he was there, for about - well,

speaker: NWS
words: two or three weeks, most of the time. He was just running over there. He

speaker: NWS
words: lived right close to us. But-fiey didn't know whether she'd pull through

speaker: NWS
words: hardly. It just got her down and everything. I never did whoop mother said,

speaker: NWS
words: the decent whoop. Said I coughed a time or two but just aTommon cough mother

speaker: NWS
words: said, there wasn't any whoop to it. And that's just the way everything went.

speaker: NWS
words: After we got out Mfest, I went to school and got the chicken pox and went home

speaker: NWS
words: and took 'em to her and she just about croaked with 'em. She was just

speaker: NWS
words: splattered with 'em - had 'em all over. And I had just about half dozen spots,

speaker: NWS
words: I guess, was about all I had on me. Just all things like that. She

speaker: NWS
words: always got the bad end of the deal.

speaker: NWS
words: But on this Christmas vacation we had the conductor to let us off at the

speaker: NWS
words: crossing and put Edna off first. There was a wind like we had here the

speaker: NWS
words: other day. And you know, the track was up high and then sloped down, you

speaker: NWS
words: know, to the ditch thing. Well, Edna just went rolling right down - end over end -

speaker: NWS
words: the wind was so strong.

speaker: ROB
words: Pulled her right over -

speaker: NWS
words: And the train would stop, why she'd just go right along kinda toward

speaker: NWS
words: the wheels but she was going toward the ditch, you know. And then by the

speaker: NWS
words: time they'd gotten mother off and she gashed over between her and the conductor,

speaker: NWS
words: and got Edna picked up and she hung on to her. And then they put me off next,

speaker: NWS
words: and I don't remember what happened. I suppose mother grabbed onto me

speaker: NWS
words: kind of, but I could pretty well hold myself. After the train puljed out, why we set

speaker: NWS
words: down

speaker: NWS
words: right on the track there and held to the rail, and kept from blowing. And we set there

speaker: NWS
words: quite a while. Dad was going to meet us, you know, when that train went

speaker: NWS
words: through. Well, he was still pounding out those carpentering,there and he was

speaker: NWS
words: still working away at it and missed the time and didn't know that the train

speaker: NWS
words: was due yet. And I can't think of that girl's name, a Swedish name. And

speaker: NWS
words: I don't know, it wasn't Hanna but I'll guess I'll call her that for convenience.

speaker: NWS
words: But she happened to be standing at the sink washing dishes and well, she

speaker: NWS
words: looked out the window and says, "Oh, Mrs. Robinson, look over there.

speaker: NWS
words: Is that the Woods, Mrs. Woods and the family over there?" And they looked

speaker: NWS
words: out there and there we set,you know, holding onto that track. And she said,

speaker: NWS
words: "Yes, for heaven sakes." She said, "I'll go over to help 'em." Mrs.

speaker: NWS
words: Robinson said, "I'll go get Mr. Woods, and i'lHtell him." And so here came

speaker: NWS
words: that girl - the name just slips me each time, but anyhow as I said, I'll call

speaker: NWS
words: her Hanna - and she came running and the wind just blowing every direction you

speaker: NWS
words: could hardly run against it or walk even. But she was used to it and she came

speaker: NWS
words: dashing over there to where we were and she got us.

speaker: NWS
words: By that time the wind had whipped our hats off, we're just pret'near striped.

speaker: NWS
words: We didn't have our coats buttoned tight and we all had new outfits. Dad had had

speaker: NWS
words: a pay check or two and we'd gotten new - Mother had got her a new jacket.

speaker: NWS
words: They wore those short jackets at that time, fitted kind. Mother had her new brown

speaker: NWS
words: jacket and her brown hat and, oh, all feathered and plum ed. I don't know.

speaker: NWS
words: It was all matched all kind of nice, and she outfitted me in a red coat, a jacket

speaker: NWS
words: and then the red hat. Because of being so windy there some times, why we

speaker: NWS
words: had veils too, and tied them on. And Edna had one of these.poke bonnets like

speaker: NWS
words: they put on little kids, you know. And it was heavy and it was wool, wooly thing ,

speaker: NWS
words: and her blue coat. So we're all fix up nice and we had mittens and everything, and

speaker: NWS
words: rubber galoshes. Anyhow, when we got off there and the wind had got us, first

speaker: NWS
words: the veil would go there and then a veil would go the other way and then the hats

speaker: NWS
words: went. "Course they weren't pinned on us but just a rubber band under the chin.

speaker: NWS
words: And by the time we had the girl lead us- it strikes that that girls name is Sophie,

speaker: NWS
words: I don't remember. But anyhow, by the time we started across the meadow there

speaker: NWS
words: to go over to the other place, why the - we had lost our rubbers or one of them,

speaker: NWS
words: I remember one rubber. And we had our pockets stuffed full of candy and gum

speaker: NWS
words: to take to a little boy, Stillman. And it had just whipped the candy and stuff out

speaker: NWS
words: of our pockets and I don't know, I think, one of the rubbers was gone or else one

speaker: NWS
words: us did have and one of us didn't have. But I think just one rubber is all that

speaker: NWS
words: left me with, I think. And I don't remember much about it. I know our

speaker: NWS
words: mittens were gone and it just whipped the little dickens right out of us pret'near.

speaker: NWS
words: So the time we had started back toward the house.

speaker: NWS
words: Then met Dad coming-he was coming on the run. By that time we'd told him what

speaker: NWS
words: had happened and he let her then take us on over to the house and he started up

speaker: NWS
words: across the track and up over the hill up on the other side of the track through

speaker: NWS
words: the sagebrueh. And he thought maybe, you know, he'd save some of our raps

speaker: NWS
words: and hats and coats and things or mittens and things. And so he'd take out and

speaker: NWS
words: get them maybe, catch them before they got ta far, caught in the brush. He

speaker: NWS
words: didn't get a thing. Not a thing could he retrieve. So - but - went back then and

speaker: NWS
words: he was sitting feeling kinda thinking he wasn't there when the train came through

speaker: NWS
words: but there might have been a few things that he might have saved. But I dorft

speaker: NWS
words: think it was very much because you didn't dare let loose of that rail...

speaker: ROB
words: He might have lost some too!

speaker: NWS
words: But didn't dare let loose of that rail or you would have went right on.

speaker: ROB
words: Did you have a good Christmas anyway?

speaker: NWS
words: Oh, heavens, an awful nice Christmas and wonderful people. Had a good time

speaker: NWS
words: but I never had had a red coat or hat or a red dress or anything. I don't think I

speaker: NWS
words: had a

speaker: NWS
words: red dress at that time because my hair was so near red that they just didn't dress

speaker: NWS
words: me in red. It just didn't look good. But I had a deep dark red - it was an

speaker: NWS
words: expensive coat and hat, and that was the first hat I had had. I just felt real

speaker: NWS
words: dressed up and then I thought I'd lost a whole lot. But we stayed, I don't know,

speaker: NWS
words: several days out there, I think, and then went back.

speaker: ROB
words: You moved on to Rexberg after a couple of years. ..

speaker: NWS
words: Well, it was - let's see, I can't remember the ages exactly. We went to

speaker: NWS
words: Rexberg. I don't know how we come to go over there. I know the small pox was

speaker: NWS
words: going in the country in Laramie there. A bunch of 'em came down with it. They

speaker: NWS
words: were afraid the men were going to come down with it on the bridge gang. And so

speaker: NWS
words: the order came down the line everybody be vaccinated. And so there was a

speaker: NWS
words: certain day set and the doctor was out there. Everybody come through the car - the

speaker: NWS
words: office car. And the doctor setting there and everybody come through with their

speaker: NWS
words: sleeve rolled up. So they got to Dad - he had his sleeve rolled up - and they

speaker: NWS
words: said, "Go on." Said, "But you didn't get him." "You don't need it because he's

speaker: NWS
words: got a scar about as big as a half dollar." He'd been vaccinated before and they

speaker: NWS
words: said anybody with a scar like that don't need it, don't need another one. And

speaker: NWS
words: so he was ready for it but he didn't have to be vaccinated but the rest of them

speaker: NWS
words: all had their vaccinations .. But after we got to Rexberg - I forgot, I

speaker: NWS
words: don't know what the people we met at - I don't know at Rexberg...

speaker: NWS
words: (End of Side A)

speaker: NWS
words: So we got acquainted there with a family by the name of Lee. I don't know

speaker: NWS
words: what he did. He worked I guess away from home most the time. I don't

speaker: NWS
words: remember him. But Mrs. Lee and they had one grown up boy, I think. He

speaker: NWS
words: was about 17 or 19 or something and there was one about 15. They just had

speaker: NWS
words: those two. And they lived over across the track from where we lived. And they -

speaker: NWS
words: we lived, oh, quite a little ways from the track I woul d say, which is about a

speaker: NWS
